critique.summary: OpenCode is an open source AI coding agent that integrates with various coding environments and supports multiple AI models from different providers. It claims to be widely used, with over 120,000 GitHub stars, 800 contributors, and 5 million monthly users, emphasizing privacy by not storing user code or context data.
mainPoints: OpenCode is an open source AI coding agent available as a desktop app, terminal interface, and IDE extension.
It supports multiple AI models from providers like Claude, GPT, Gemini, and over 75 others via Models.dev.
Features include multi-session support, LSP integration, sharing session links, and login options for GitHub Copilot and ChatGPT Plus/Pro accounts.
The project claims significant community engagement with over 120,000 GitHub stars, 800 contributors, and 5 million monthly developers using it.
OpenCode prioritizes privacy by not storing users' code or context data.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about OpenCode being an open source AI coding agent with multi-platform support and integration with various AI models are consistent with typical features of similar tools. The GitHub statistics (120,000 stars, 800 contributors) and user base (5 million monthly developers) are plausible but should be verified directly on the project's GitHub repository and usage analytics. The privacy claim aligns with open source principles but requires confirmation from the project's privacy policy or independent audits. Overall, the article's claims are generally credible but lack external citations or multiple perspectives to fully verify the scale of usage and privacy assurances.
considerations: The article does not provide external sources or third-party reviews to validate the usage statistics or privacy claims.
It does not mention potential limitations or challenges users might face with OpenCode, such as model performance variability or compatibility issues.
There is no discussion of how OpenCode compares to other AI coding agents in terms of features, accuracy, or user experience.
The article lacks detailed information on security measures beyond the privacy claim.
No information is provided about the governance or funding of the OpenCode project, which could impact its sustainability.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around OpenCode, an open-source AI coding agent, with users sharing mixed experiences regarding its features, development pace, security, and usability. While many appreciate its extensibility, client-server architecture, and free models, concerns about security, resource usage, and UI complexity are common.

comments.author: jFriedensreich
comments.text: opencode stands out as one of the few agents with a proper client server architecture that allows something like openchambers great vscode extension so its possible to seamlessly switch between tui, vscode, webapp, desktop app. i think there is hardly a usable alternative for most coding agent usecases (assuming agents from model providers are a no go, they cannot be allowed to own the tools AND the models). But its also far from perfect: the webui is secretly served from their servers instead of locally for no reason. worse the fallback route gets also sent to their servers so any unknown request to opencode api ends up being sent to opencode servers potentially leaking data. the security defaults are horrific, its impossible to use it safely outside a controlled container. it will just serve your whole hard drive via rest endpoint and not constrain to project folders. the share feature uploading your conversations to their servers is also so weirdly communicated and implemented that it leaves a bad taste. I dont think this will become much better until the agent ecosystem is more modular and less monolith, acp, a2a and mcp need to become good enough so tools, prompts, skills, subagent setups and workflow engines and UIs are completely swappable and the agent core has to only focus on the essentials like runtime and glue architecture. i really hope we dont see all of these grow into full agent oses with artificial lock in effects and big effort buy in.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and nuanced critique of OpenCode's architecture and security issues, highlighting real concerns about data leakage and unsafe defaults, which are corroborated by other users.

comments.author: logicprog
comments.text:
OpenCode was the first open source agent I used, and my main workhorse after experimenting briefly with Claude Code and realizing the potential of agentic coding. Due to that, and because it's a popular an open source alternative, I want to be able to recommend it and be enthusiastic about it. The problem for me is that the development practices of the people that are working on it are suboptimal at best; they're constantly releasing at an extremely high cadence, where they don't even spend the time to test or fix things (or even build a proper list of changes for each release), and they add, remove, refine, change, fix, and break features constantly at that accelerated pace.

More than that, it's an extremely large and complex TypeScript code base — probably larger and more complex than it needs to be — and (partly as a result) it's fairly resource inefficient (often uses 1GB of RAM or more. For a TUI).

On top of that, at least I personally find the TUI to be overbearing and a little bit buggy, and the agent to be so full of features that I don't really need — also mildly buggy — that it sort of becomes hard to use and remember how everything is supposed to work and interact.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a balanced critique based on personal experience, pointing out valid issues with release cadence, code complexity, and resource use that align with other users' observations.

comments.author: solarkraft
comments.text:
I found out about OpenCode through the Anthropic feud. I now spend most of my AI time in it, both at work and at home. It turns out to be pretty great for general chat too, with the ability to easily integrate various tools you might need (search being the top one of course).

I have things to criticize about it, their approach to security and pulling in code being my main one, but over all it’s the most complete solution I’ve found.

They have a server/client architecture, a client SDK, a pretty good web UI and use pretty standard technologies.

The extensibility story is good and just seems like the right paradigms mostly, with agents, skills, plugins and providers.

They also ship very fast, both for good and bad, I’ve personally enjoyed the rapid improvements (~2 days from criticizing not being able to disable the default provider in the web ui to being able to).

I think OpenCode has a pretty bright future and so far I think that my issues with it should be pretty fixable. The amount of tasteful choices they’ve made dwarfs the few untasteful ones for me so far.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a positive yet realistic view, acknowledging security issues while emphasizing the platform's strengths and rapid development, contributing a balanced perspective.

comments.author: jee599
comments.text:
The security concerns here are real but not unique to OpenCode. Most AI coding agents have the same fundamental problem: they need broad file system access to be useful, but that access surface is also the attack surface. The config-from-web issue is particularly bad because it's essentially remote code execution through prompt injection.

What I'd want to see from any of these tools is a clear permissions model — which files the agent can read vs write, whether it can execute commands, and an audit log of what it actually did. Claude Code's hooks system at least gives you deterministic guardrails before/after agent actions, but it's still early days for this whole category.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully discusses systemic security challenges in AI coding agents and suggests practical improvements, reflecting a deep understanding of the domain.

comments.author: planckscnst
comments.text:
I love OpenCode! I wrote a plugin that adds two tools: prune and retrieve. Prune lets the LLM select messages to remove from the conversation and replace with a summary and key terms. The retrieve tool lets it get those original messages back in case they're needed. I've been livestreaming the development and using it on side projects to make sure it's actually effective... And it turns out it really is! It feels like working with an infinite context window.

https://www.youtube.com/live/z0JYVTAqeQM?si=oLvyLlZiFLTxL7p0
comments.analysis: This comment adds valuable information about extensibility and practical enhancements, supported by evidence of active development and usage.

comments.author: brendanmc6
comments.text:
I’ve been extraordinarily productive with this, their $10 Go plan, and a rigorous spec-driven workflow. Haven’t touched Claude in 2 months.

I sprinkle in some billed API usage to power my task-planner and reviewer subagents (both use GPT 5.4 now).

The ability to switch models is very useful and a great learning experience. GLM, Kimi and their free models surprised me. Not the best, not perfect, but still very productive. I would be a wary shareholder if I owned a stake in the frontier labs… that moat seems to be shrinking fast.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a user perspective on pricing, productivity, and model flexibility, highlighting practical benefits and market implications.

comments.author: zkmon
comments.text:
OpenCode works awesome for me. The BigPickle model is all I want. I do not throw some large work at the agent that requires lot of reasoning, thinking or decision making. It's my role to chop the work down to bite-size and ask the fantastic BigPickle to just do the damn coding or bit of explaining. It works very well with interactive sessions with small tasks. Not giving something to work over night.

I used Claude with paid subscription and codex as well and settled to OpenCode with free models.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a focused use case and preference rationale, emphasizing effective use of free models for manageable tasks.

comments.author: ndom91
comments.text:
Since this is blowing up, gonna plug my opencode/claude-code plugin that allows you to annotate LLMs plans like a Google doc with strikethroughs, comments, etc. and loop with your agent until you're happy with the plan.

https://github.com/ndom91/open-plan-annotator
comments.analysis: This comment contributes a constructive tool that enhances agent interaction and planning, showing community-driven innovation.

comments.author: sebastianconcpt
comments.text:
OpenCode is the almost good IDE I need.

What does well: helps context switching by using one window to control many repos with many worktrees each.

What can do better? It's putting AI too much in control? What if I want to edit a function myself in the workspace I'm working on? or select a snippet and refer that in the promp? without that I feel it's missing a non-negotiable feature.
comments.analysis: The comment thoughtfully critiques UI/UX balance, highlighting a potential overreliance on AI control that may hinder manual developer workflows.

comments.author: hmcdona1
comments.text:
Can someone explain how Claude Code can instantly determine what file I have open and what lines I have selected in VS Code even if it's just running in a VS Code terminal instance, yet I cannot for the life of me get OpenCode to come anywhere close to that same experience?

The OpenCode docs suggest its possible, but it only works with their extension (not in an already open VS Code terminal) with a very specific keyboard shortcut and only barely at that.
comments.analysis: This comment points out a usability gap in OpenCode compared to competitors, providing a specific example of integration limitations.
